coredata.dc:title: Fe–Co–Fe prussian blue analogues loaded nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots for effective epinephrine detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chu, Mingyue
Wang, Ying
Xin, Jianjiao
O'Halloran, Kevin P.
Liu, Yikun
Song, Daozheng
Yang, Guixin
Ma, Huiyuan
Pang, Haijun
Wang, Xinming
coredata.dc:description: Epinephrine (Ep) is an important neurotransmitter, which plays an important role in the nervous system and glycogen metabolism of living organisms. Hence, a novel NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA composite with FeCoFe-Prussian blue analogues (PBA) as the core and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as the shell was constructed by a one-pot hydrothermal method, and it was used for the efficient detection of Ep. As a good electroactive material, NCQDs in the composite not only improved the weak conductivity of FeCoFe-PBA, but also limited the self-aggregation of FeCoFe-PBA, and formed a uniform shell on FeCoFe-PBA. The heterogeneous structure formed between the core and shell layer resulting in NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA nanocomposites with more active sites, electron transport channels and a larger effective surface area. Further, under optimal conditions, the electrochemical method was used to evaluate the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor, and the results revealed that the sensor had exceptional sensing performance for Ep, with an excellent linear range from 0.01 to 306.7 μM and a low detection limit of 0.002 μM. Simultaneously, the practicality, repeatability, and stability tests yielded positive results, confirming the feasibility of practical development and application of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prussian blue analogues
Nitrogen-carbon doped quantum dots
Core-shell structure
Electrochemical sensor
Epinephrine

Electrochemical surface area and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
The impact of different scan rates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) FeCoFe-PBA and (b) NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA. TEM images of (c) NCQDs, (d) and (e) NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA. (f) HRTEM image of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA.
(a) UV spectra and (b) Raman spectra of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD pattern of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA. (c) ∼ (f) the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p, Fe 2p, C 1s and N 1s in NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA.

(a) CV curves of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s in KCl/[Fe(CN)6]3-/4- mixture solution (0.1 M/5 mM). (b) The peak current (anodic/cathodic) corresponds to the linear equation of the square root of the scan rate. (c) CV curves of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA and NCQs/FeCoFe-PBA in KCl/[Fe(CN)6]3-/4- mixture solution (0.1 M/5 mM). (d) EIS curves of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA and NCQs/FeCoFe-PBA.

The CV curves of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA ranging from 10 mV/s to 80 mV/s in PBS containing 100 μM Ep. Insets: (Ⅰ) The correlation between scan rate and response current, (Ⅱ) The correlation between logarithm of the sweep rate and the response potential.

(a) DPV curves for bare GCE, FeCoFe-PBA, NCQDs and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA in 0.1 M PBS solution with 50 μM Ep and pH = 7. (b) NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA DPV curves in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM and 50 μM Ep at pH = 7, respectively. (c) The linear relationship between response current and Ep concentration.

(a) DPV response curves of 60 μM Ep on NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor in PBS solution with pH levels ranging from 4 to 10. (b) The impact of different pH values on the response current and response potential of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor.
(a) DPV curves of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) with varying Ep concentrations. (b) The calibration graph for response current vs. Ep concentration.

DPV curves for NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 7) for (a) 50 μM Ep, and (b) 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM and 50 μM Ep, in the prescene of NaCl, NH4Cl, CuCl2, FeCl2, KCl, CaCl2, ascorbic acid, adenosine, glucose, dopamine, tryptophan (Trp), uric acid, dopamine, tyrosine, folic acid and hypoxanthine with 50 times the concentration of Ep.

(a) The same NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor's DPV curve with 50 μM Ep was repeated five times. (b) Five different NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensors' DPV curve with 50 μM Ep. (c) i-t curve of the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensors with 100 μM Ep after 12 h of continuous scanning. (d) Plots of stability of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA for longer time periods.
The possible reaction process of Ep on NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA.
Evaluation of the electrochemical capabilities of the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor with several published Ep sensors.
Determination of Ep in human urine sample.

head.ce:title: Fe–Co–Fe prussian blue analogues loaded nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots for effective epinephrine detection

Harbin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science and Technology, Georgia Gwinnett College, Lawrenceville, GA, 30043, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science and Technology, Georgia Gwinnett College, Lawrenceville, GA 30043, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Epinephrine (Ep) is an important neurotransmitter, which plays an important role in the nervous system and glycogen metabolism of living organisms. Hence, a novel NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA composite with FeCoFe-Prussian blue analogues (PBA) as the core and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as the shell was constructed by a one-pot hydrothermal method, and it was used for the efficient detection of Ep. As a good electroactive material, NCQDs in the composite not only improved the weak conductivity of FeCoFe-PBA, but also limited the self-aggregation of FeCoFe-PBA, and formed a uniform shell on FeCoFe-PBA. The heterogeneous structure formed between the core and shell layer resulting in NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA nanocomposites with more active sites, electron transport channels and a larger effective surface area. Further, under optimal conditions, the electrochemical method was used to evaluate the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor, and the results revealed that the sensor had exceptional sensing performance for Ep, with an excellent linear range from 0.01 to 306.7 μM and a low detection limit of 0.002 μM. Simultaneously, the practicality, repeatability, and stability tests yielded positive results, confirming the feasibility of practical development and application of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Epinephrine (Ep) is a key neurotransmitter that has a substantial impact on the neurological system and glycogen metabolism [1–4]. It can elevate blood sugar levels by increasing glycogen breakdown in the liver and fat metabolization in cells, giving more energy to the body and thus speeding up heart rate, respiration, and blood flow [5–7]. Many diseases are closely related to Ep concentrations, such as hypertension, schizophrenia, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease [8–11]. According to research, Ep concentration in biological fluids and neurological tissues is an important diagnostic basis for many disorders, thus developing an effective method to monitor Ep content is critical [12–14]. At present, a variety of Ep detection methods have been reported, such as polarography, capillary electrophoresis, photodynamic method, high performance liquid chromatography, flow injection method, mass spectrometry [15–23]. These methods have been successfully used for the detection of Ep, but they still have some unavoidable disadvantages, such as the instrument is expensive and large, the detection method is time-consuming, and the experimental conditions are demanding. In contrast, electrochemical sensing technology stands out among many detection methods for its advantages of convenient portability, simple operation and fast response speed [24–28]. Currently, the most commonly used technique for testing electrochemical sensors is cyclic voltammetry (CV). However, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is often preferred for the detection of trace levels of organic and inorganic substances. This preference arises from its ability to enhance analytical signals by effectively eliminating non-Faradaic currents, which results in improved resolution and sensitivity compared to CV [28].

Prussian blue analogues (PBA) are a class of classical three-dimensional open metal-organic framework materials with the molecular formula PxD[R(CN)6]·nH2O (P for insertion ions, D and R for transition metal ions) [29]. With the development of science and technology, PBA has been widely used in charge transfer, environmental cleanup, drug delivery, batteries, energy separation, online monitoring, energy storage and other fields [30]. PBA has been used as a promising electrochemical sensing material due to its unique properties [31–35]: (Ⅰ) PBA has a zeolite frame structure with a high specific surface area, which makes it stable; (Ⅱ) The open framework of PBA can provide a rich three-dimensional channel for the transfer of various electron ions when used as electrode materials; (Ⅲ) Compared with single metal framework compounds, PBA can possess various metal active centers with high electrocatalytic activity; (Ⅳ) The synthesis procedure is straightforward with relatively cheap raw material cost. However, its poor electrical conductivity and self-aggregation problems hinder its development in the electrochemical field as electrode materials. Therefore overcoming these issues remains a work in progress.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a promising nanomaterial that have been widely used in various fields due to their small particle sizes, high conductivity, low cost, excellent stability, and high biocompatibility [36–40]. It is well known that N-doping methods can improve the electron transport of semiconductor materials, increase the active site and quantum yield [41,42]. In particular, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), as an important member of the quantum dot family, have more surface and edge structural defects, higher conductivity, better dispersion, as well as superior electrochemical activity than CQDs alone, and are widely used in the domains of bioimaging, photocatalysis, electrical sensing [43,44].

Inspired by these findings, FeCoFe-PBA with a unique nanocube morphology was chosen as the carrier of NCQDs to fabricate electrochemical composite materials NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA for determining Ep. NCQDs were created using urea and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen sources and ammonium citrate as the carbon source by a simple and environmentally friendly reflux technique with a high yield. In prepared NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, FeCoFe-PBA possesses many intrinsic merits of a large specific surface area, homogeneous dispersion of the metal active site, and efficient electronic transmission channels. Meanwhile, NCQDs were uniformly loaded on FeCoFe-PBA to form the core-shell structure, which not only successfully overcomes the self-agglomeration problem during the synthesis of FeCoFe-PBA but also increases the electrical conductivity of the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA. Therefore, the prepared NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor exhibited good sensing performance for Ep due to its large specific surface area, a large number of active sites, multiple redox origin pairs, excellent structural stability, and an efficient channel for electron transport.

FeCl2·4H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, K3[Co(CN)6], PVP, urea, ethylenediamine, ammonium citrate, epinephrine, adenosine, tyrosine, tryptophan, hypoxanthine, glucose, dopamine, folic acid, uric acid and ascorbic acid, were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. The chemicals used in the analysis experiment are all analytically pure. In all experiments, deionized water with a resistivity of 18 MΩ was used as the solvent.

The FT-IR absorption spectra were obtained using a Bruker Tensor II spectrometer. XPS was performed by a ESCALAB-MKII spectrometer. The XRD pattern were done with the equipment of X'Pert-Pro MPD from Netherlands. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were tested by a S-4300 SEM microscope. TEM images were obtained on an H-7650 TEM microscope. The electrochemical experiments were performed by the electrochemical workstation (CHI 760D) in 0.1 M PBS, at room temperature. Platinum wire electrode was used as an auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl chloride electrode was used as a reference electrode and the prepared electrode was used as the working electrode.

The following steps were taken to synthesize NCQDs [45]: 2 mL ethylenediamine, 1.95 g ammonium citrate and 0.80 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL H2O. The solution was transferred to a 30 mL reactor and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. The cooled reaction solution was then filtered on a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane. Finally, the filtrate was transferred to the dialysis bag, and the water was changed every 6 h. After seven days, the remaining solution in the dialysis bag was the CNQDs solution.

0.0358 g FeCl2·4H2O, 0.0428 g CoCl2·6H2O, 1.2 g PVP and 5 mL NCQDs were added to 40 mL H2O at 65 °C successively, and then stirred for 30 min. Then, a solution of 0.0659 g K3[Fe(CN)6] in 40 ml H2O was added drop by drop into the solution generated in the first step, and stirred for 30 min. Finally, the obtained solution was poured into a beaker and the precipitated product was NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA after aging at room temperature for 24 h. FeCoFe-PBA was synthesized in the same way, but without NCQDs. Scheme 1 depicted the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA preparation process diagram.

The bare GCE (d = 3 mm) was treated according to the methods reported in the literature [46]. First, 6 mg NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA was added to 950 μL deionized water and 50 μL Nafion (0.5 wt %) and sonicated for 1 h to obtain a uniform suspension. Then, 5 μL of the suspension was precisely obtained with a micropipette and carefully transferred to the surface of the GCE. For consistency, NCQDs/GCE and FeCoFe-PBA/GCE were produced according to the same procedure.

The SEM images in Fig. 1 (a) and (b) exhibited the morphology and structure of FeCoFe-PBA and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, respectively. FeCoFe-PBA has a uniform and distinct cube morphology with an average size of around 200 nm, but agglomeration occurs among the nanoparticles. Compared to FeCoFe-PBA, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA in Fig. 1 (b) showed similar morphology and size but with the improved property of reduced aggregation. However, no visible NCQDs nanoparticles were observed in SEM in Fig. 1 (b), which was probably due to the fact that the particle size of NCQDs was too small to detect by SEM. The morphologies of NCQDs and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA were further explored by TEM showed in Fig. 1 (c), (d), and (e), respectively. Fig. 1 (c) showed a uniform and distributed NCQDs nanoparticle with a particle size of less than 10 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 1(d) and (e), NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA exhibited a core-shell structure with NCQDs uniformly loaded on cubic FeCoFe-PBA. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA depicted two parallel lattice fringe pairs with spacings of 0.36 and 0.46 nm, which was associated with the crystal faces of NCQDs (002) and FeCoFe-PBA (200) [47], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis, Raman, FT-IR, XRD and XPS spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectrum of NCQDs in Fig. 2 (a) exhibits a distinct absorption peak at approximately 240 nm, which is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds. Additionally, a shoulder peak is observed at 350 nm, corresponding to the n-π∗ transition of CO bonds [45]. These characteristic absorption features indicate the presence of quantum dot structures and confirm the successful synthesis of NCQDs. The spectrum of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA shows similar characterized absorptions to NCQDs, confirming the structural and optical properties of NCQDs are well-retained after integration with FeCoFe-PBA.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to investigate the structural features of the synthesized products at different stages, and the corresponding results are displayed in Fig. 2(b). The Raman spectrum of NCQDs exhibits two characteristic peaks located at 1356 cm⁻1 and 1610 cm⁻1, corresponding to the disordered D band and the crystalline G band of carbon materials, respectively [48]. For FeCoFe-PBA, the Raman spectrum shows distinct characteristic peaks at 2182 cm⁻1, 1387 cm⁻1, and 1576 cm⁻1. The peak at 2182 cm⁻1 is attributed to the C N stretching vibration associated with cobalt ferricyanide, while the peaks at 1387 cm⁻1 and 1576 cm⁻1 correspond to the disordered D band and crystalline G band of carbon materials, respectively [29,33,49]. These features confirm the successful synthesis of FeCoFe-PBA with its characteristic coordination structure. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA exhibits all the characteristic peaks of both NCQDs and FeCoFe-PBA, including the D and G bands of carbon and the C N stretching vibration. This result confirms the successful integration of NCQDs and FeCoFe-PBA into a composite material. The presence of these distinct features suggests that the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA composite retains the structural characteristics of its individual components, indicating effective preparation of the target material with potential synergistic properties for enhanced performance.

FT-IR was applied to characterize the functional groups of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA, and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA as shown in Fig. 3 (a) . The NCQDs spectrum shows peaks at 1637 and 636 cm−1, belonging to the bending vibrations of N–H and C–H, respectively [50]. In the spectrum of FeCoFe-PBA, the stretching vibration of C N, the bending vibration of –OH, and the stretching vibration of C–N, respectively, were represented by the peaks at 2170, 1615, and 598 cm−1 [51,52]. The characteristic peaks of typical functional groups of both NCQDs and FeCoFe-PBA could be observed in NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, demonstrating the successful loading of NCQDs on FeCoFe-PBA.

As shown in Fig. 3 (b), the crystallization state of the synthesized NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA, and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA was investigated using XRD. The peak at 22.5° in the NCQDs spectrum is attributed to the (002) crystal plane of NCQDs, providing strong evidence for the successful fabrication of NCQDs [53]. The FeCoFe-PBA XRD pattern revealed eight distinct peaks at 17.5°, 24.9°, 28.9°, 35.4°, 39.6°, 43.9°, 50.6°, and 54.9° corresponding to crystal faces (200), (220), (400), (420), (424), (440), (600), and (620), respectively [51,54]. At the same time, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA inherited the nearly identical crystal peak positions of NCQDs and FeCoFe-PBA, demonstrating that the successful loading of NCQDs on FeCoFe-PBA did not disrupt both crystal structures.

In order to better evaluate the elemental makeup of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, XPS was carried out. Fig. 3 (c) through (e) depict high-resolution maps of Co 2p, Fe 2p, C 1s, and N 1s, respectively. Peaks in the Co 2p spectra at 802.7/797.4 eV and 784.9/781.9 eV are attributed to p1/2 and p3/2 orbitals, respectively, while peaks at 807.3 eV and 788.4 eV are the satellite peaks of Co [51,55]. In the spectrum of Fe 2p, the p1/2 and p3/2 orbitals are located at 724.1/721.4 eV and 710.1/708.4 eV, respectively, and the satellite peaks are positioned at 727.4 eV and 713.1 eV [51,56]. Four peaks located at 287.9 eV, 286.1 eV, 284.9 eV, and 284.2 eV are observed in the XPS spectrum of C 1s, attributed to C–O, C–N, C–C, and C N, respectively [47]. The peaks at 400.5 eV, 399.8 eV, 398.7 eV, and 397.7 eV in the N spectrum correspond to N–H, N–C, NC, and N C, respectively [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical surface area and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The effective electrode surface area of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA is evaluated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The CV curves recorded at various scan rates are shown in Fig. 4 (a) , and the linear relationship between the peak current (Ipa) and the square root of the scan rate (v 1/2) is presented in Fig. 4(b). The linear equation is Ipa = 14.51 v 1/2 - 12.80, R2 = 0.997, indicating a diffusion-controlled redox process. Additionally, the peak separation between oxidation and reduction is approximately 60 mV, which further supports the reversibility of the electron transfer reaction. The effective electrochemical surface area of the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA electrode was calculated using the Randles-Sevcik equation [57,58]: Ipa = 2.69 × 105 n3/2 A D0 1/2 C v 1/2, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, where n = 1 (one-electron transfer process), D0 = 7.6 × 10−6 cm2/s (diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4),C = 5 mM (concentration of the redox probe), and v is the scan rate. Based on the data, the effective electrochemical surface area of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA was calculated to be 3.91 cm2, significantly higher than that of the bare GCE (0.0707 cm2). This enhancement is attributed to the uniform and distributed three-dimensional yolk core-shell structure of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, which provides a larger active surface area and facilitates electron transfer. Lastly, we note that the peak separation increases as the scan rate increases, such behavior is due to kinetic limitations, which result in a non-ideal response at elevated scan rates. While the system demonstrates reversible behavior under standard conditions, deviations at higher scan rates are consistent with diffusion-controlled kinetics.

Fig. 4 (c) depicts the CV curves of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA, and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA in KCl/[Fe (CN)6]3-/4- mixture solution (0.1 M/5 mM). NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA show a pair of redox peaks with highest peak currents and smallest spacings compared to NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA, further confirming superior electron transport ability and redox performance of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA. The electrochemical impedance spectra of NCQDs, FeCoFe-PBA, and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA were performed and shown in Fig. 4 (d). The high frequency region of the Nyquist diagram reveals to the electron transfer resistance of the material. According to the impedance fitting results from Fig. 4 (d), the resistances of FeCoFe-PBA, NCQDs, and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA are estimated to be 6.4, 1.7, and 1.3 kΩ, respectively, which verified that the composite material NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA has lower resistance and faster electron transfer rate than its individual components. The noteworthy electrochemical performances of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA nanomaterials are due to its unique core-shell heterostructure, as well as the synergistic effect of NCQDs and FeCoFe-PBA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of different scan rates
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 exhibits the CV curve of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA in PBS solution containing 100 μM Ep from 10 to 80 mV sweep speed, and its insets (Ⅰ) and (Ⅱ) shows a linear relationship between scan rate and response current, as well as a link between sweep rate logarithm and response potential. It can be seen from inset (Ⅰ) in Fig. 5 that the response current increased linearly with increasing scanning rate, reflecting that the material's electron transfer reaction is a regulated process of surface adsorption. Furthermore, in Fig. 5 inset (Ⅱ), the equation for the linear relationship between the sweep rate's logarithm and the response potential is as follows: Epa = 0.039 + 0.24, R2 = 0.991. The electron transfer number of Ep in NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA response can be determined using Laviron theory [49,59]: Epa = E0 - (2.3RT/ɑnF)[log(RTKs/ɑnF)-logv] (T = 298 K, R = 8.314 J/K·mol, F represents Faraday's constant, n represents the number of electrons and ɑ represents the transfer coefficient). The indicated transfer coefficient ɑ can be determined using the equation below: Epa - Epa/2 = 1.857RT/ɑF [59]. It can be deduced from the previous equation that ɑ = 0.96, n = 1.86. According to the above results, Ep's response process on NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA is a two-electron transfer reaction, which is compatible with Ep's reported reaction mechanism, and the reaction of Ep might be depicted in Scheme 2 .

Initially, the electrochemical sensing properties of bare GCE, FeCoFe-PBA, NCQDs, and NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA towards Ep was investigated in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 50 μM Ep using DPV method is presented in Fig. 6 (a) . From the DPV curve of the bare GCE, it can be observed that there is a weak response peak current at 0.42 V. The increased response peak current was observed in DPV curves of FeCoFe-PBA and NCQDs at a lower potential of 0.38 and 0.31 V, respectively. As expected, the DPV curve of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA exhibited a significantly higher response peak current at a more negative potential of 0.26 V compared to bare GCE, FeCoFe-PBA and NCQD.

The main reasons for this phenomenon are as follows: (I) Firstly, the cavity structure of FeCoFe-PBA allows the right amount of alkali metal ions (such as Na+ and K+) to enter and leave, thereby improving electron transport, conductivity, and electrical activity. Co and Fe ions function as a large number of active metal sites in FeCoFe-PBA and exhibit high redox activity, resulting in rapid electron transfer kinetics. However, as an electrode material, FeCoFe-PBA by itself will be limited by the defect of agglomeration in the electrochemical test, which not only blocks the active site, but also restricts the electron transfer channel of the detected substance during the redox process. (Ⅱ) Secondly, CNQDs have high electrical conductivity, abundant edge structure defects, and can show excellent electrochemical activity in electrochemical tests. However, the particle size is very small, and when used as a single electrode material, the stability is poor and the recycling rate is poor. (Ⅲ) Therefore, in situ CNQDs deposited on the surface of FeCoFe-PBA can not only effectively alleviate the self-agglomerating effect, but also expose a large number of surface active sites to CNQDs/FeCoFe-PBA and increase the electrochemical active area. The unique core-shell structure of CNQDs/FeCoFe-PBA can make FeCoFe-PBA as a stable semiconductor support substrate and electron transfer medium, and the resulting heterostructure provides more electron transfer channels for the detection of Ep.

To summarize, this remarkable improvement is attributed to intrinsic merits and synergistic effect of NCQDs and FeCoFe-PBA, together with homogenously scattered yolk core-shell heterostructure of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, which gives this material a large specific surface area, multiple active sites, multiple redox source pairs, good structural stability, and an effective channel for the electron transfer. As a result, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA showed superior electrocatalytic activity towards Ep with the lowest response potential and the highest response peak current.

NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA DPV curves in 0.1 M pH = 7 PBS solution containing various concentrations of Ep was performed and is illustrated in Fig. 6 (b). It can be found that the response peak current linearly rose at 0.26 V as the concentration of Ep increased, and the linear relationship between the response peak current and Ep concentrations is depicted Fig. 6 (c), which demonstrates that the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor can be used in detecting Ep.

In general, since the pH of the buffer solution has an effect on the response of the detected object on the sensor, it is critical to investigate the ideal detection conditions for Ep on the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor. Fig. 7 (a) depicts the DPV curve of 60 μM Ep on the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor in the buffer solution with different pH from 4 to 10. Fig. 7 (b) exhibits the connection between response current and response potential as a function of pH. When the pH increases from 4 to 7, the response current of Ep at the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor steadily increases, reaching its maximum value at pH = 7. However, in the pH range of 7–10, the Ep response current gradually decreases with the increase of pH value. As a result, PBS solution with pH = 7 was chosen to use for Ep detection at the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor. In addition, the linear relationship equation between the response potential of Ep and the pH of PBS solution in Fig. 7 (b) is as follows: EEp = 0.6479–0.055 pH, R2 = 0.99. The slope of 0.055 is quite close to Nernst's expected value of 0.059 [59], indicating that the Ep responds to the PBA sensor with the same number of electrons and protons. Combined with the conclusion that the reaction of Ep on the modified electrode surface is a two-electron transfer number reaction from the influence tests of different scanning rates, it can be concluded that the response reaction of Ep on NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA follows a double electron and double proton transfer. The specific possible reaction process was inferred as follows.

The selectivity of the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor toward Ep was performed by using the DPV technique in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH = 7) containing Ep and typical interfering substances of NaCl, NH4Cl, CuCl2, FeCl2, KCl, CaCl2, ascorbic acid, adenosine, glucose, dopamine, tryptophan (Trp), uric acid, dopamine, tyrosine, folic acid and hypoxanthine with 50 times the concentration of Ep. When co-existing with the above interfering substances, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA showed a strong response current peak to Ep at 0.26 V, but only a weak response current peak to Trp at 0.8 V, as shown in the DPV curve in Fig. 9 (a) , and the two response current peaks were far apart, which could be clearly identified. At the same time, the DPV curve in Fig. 9 (b) indicated that the current response of Ep on NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA still increased linearly with the increase of Ep concentration when co-existing with the above-mentioned interfering substances. In conclusion, the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor exhibited outstanding selectivity for detecting Ep, which was conducive to the popularization and implementation of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor.

To find out the repeatability of the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor, the DPV curve in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 50 μM Ep with pH = 6 was evaluated five times, as shown in Fig. 10 (a) . It was clear that the response current and response potential in the five DPV curves were almost unchanged, confirming the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor's consistent repeatability. Fig. 10 (b) depicts DPV current signal testing of 50 μM Ep by five distinct NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensors prepared in the same test conditions. There is almost the same response current and response potential which indicate the reproducability of the sensor. For the purpose of exploring the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor's longevity, i-t response test in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7) solution containing 100 μM Ep was constantly scanned for 12 h at 0.26 V, as illustrated in Fig. 10 (c). After up to 12 h of testing, the Ep's response current curve stayed as a flat line with nearly no change, confirming that the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor has the stability of practical applications. The sensor was stored at room temperature, and DPV tests were performed every 7 days. The test results after 35 days are shown in Fig. 10(d). After 35 days, the response current remained above 90 % of its initial value, indicating that the prepared NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor exhibits excellent long-term stability.

The standard addition method was used to identify human urine samples doped with Ep in order to assess the feasibility of NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor for the determination of Ep in real samples. The urine utilized in the experiment was obtained with the donor's informed consent. Urine samples were diluted 20 times in PBS solution with pH = 7, and then Ep of known concentration was added to the solution, and then the Ep content was detected using the DPV method with the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor. The relevant results are provided in Table 2 . The Ep recovery values obtained ranged from 99.30 % to 100.60 %, demonstrating that the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor has outstanding feasibility toward Ep in actual samples.

A unique composite material, NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA, was developed by using FeCoFe-PBA as a carrier to load NCQDs for efficient Ep detection. NCQDs were uniformly loaded outside FeCoFe-PBA to form a unique core-shell structure, which solved the problems of easy agglomeration and poor electrical conductivity of FeCoFe-PBA. The synergistic effect of FeCoFe-PBA and NCQDs enabled the NCQDs/FeCoFe-PBA sensor to display abundant active sites, large specific surface area, and rapid electron transport channels. For Ep detection, the sensor exhibited remarkable electrochemical sensing performance, as well as a very broad linear range (0.01–306.7 μM), remarkably low detection limit (0.003 μM), outstanding repeatability, good reproducibility, and superb stability. In summary, the strategy demonstrated in this work provides fresh ideas and insights for the development of sensing materials.

Mingyue Chu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ying Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianjiao Xin: Software. Kevin P. O'Halloran: Writing – review & editing. Yikun Liu: Data curation. Daozheng Song: Formal analysis. Guixin Yang: Writing – review & editing. Huiyuan Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Haijun Pang: Writing – review & editing. Xinming Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Major Research Plan National Natural Science Foundation of China (92061102), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171059).

Ahmadi, Fabrication of a fast, simple and sensitive voltammetric sensor for the simultaneous determination of 4-amino-hippuric acid and uric acid using a functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotube modified glassy carbon electrode, Anal. Methods 4 (2012) 1825-1832.

Wang, A free-standing and flexible phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped three-dimensional reticular porous carbon frameworks encapsulated cobaltphosphide with superior performance for nitrite detection in drinking water and sausage samples, Sensors & Actuators: B. Chemical 321 (2020) 12854.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly efficient electrochemical oxygen evolution reaction catalyst constructed from a S-treated two-dimensional Prussian blue analogue

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel nanocomposites sensor for epinephrine detection in the presence of uric acids and ascorbic acids

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced electrocatalytic performance of an ultrafine AuPt nanoalloy framework embedded in graphene towards epinephrine sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. D. Thanh, J. Balamurugan, N. T. Tuana, H. Jeong, S. H. Lee, N. H. Kim, J. H. Lee, Enhanced electrocatalytic performance of an ultrafine AuPt nanoalloy framework embedded in graphene towards epinephrine sensing, Biosensors and Bioelectronics 89 (2017) 750-757.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.X. Qiao, H.Q. Luo, N.B. Li, Fast electrocatalytic determination of methimazole at an activated glassy carbon electrode, Colloids Surf. B 62 (2008) 31-35.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.I. Khan, K.K. Tadi, R.R. Chillawar, R.V. Motghare, Interfacing electrochemically reduced graphene oxide with poly(erichrome black T) for simultaneous determination of epinephrine, uric acid and folic acid, J. Electrochem. Soc. 165 (2018) 804-813.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous electrochemical determination of dopamine and epinephrine using gold nanocrystals capped with graphene quantum dots in a silica network
